academic_profile: 4.95 weighted GPA on a full IB Diploma track, the hardest path her school offers: Math HL, English A1 HL, Theatre Arts HL, Physics, Spanish, Theory of Knowledge, extended essay in Global Politics. SAT 1540 in one sitting (800 reading and writing, 740 math). SAT Subject Tests: Literature 780, Math Level 2 770. APs so far: Psychology 5, Statistics 5, English Language 5, World History 4, Human Geography 4, with Calculus BC, English Literature, and Spanish Language this year. National Merit Semifinalist, Finalist decision pending. AP Scholar with Distinction, Brown University Book Award, attends on her school's full academic honors scholarship.
academic_strengths: Writing and English above all (800s on the verbal SAT sections, 780 Literature). Humanities and social sciences generally. Public speaking and argument. Carries IB Math HL, the hardest math track, with a 770 Math Level 2 to match. Runs three organizations on top of the full IB diploma and hasn't blinked.
academic_weaknesses: Spanish is the definite weak spot; it's the class she sweats. The other real weakness is overcommitment; sleep is the casualty.
extracurriculars: B'nai B'rith Youth Organization International Chair (4 years, 15 hrs/week: coordinated leaders from 30 countries, raised over $45K for the philanthropic fund, built curricula for an international service trip). Re-instituted and chaired an international coalition of Jewish teen movements. Theater all four years: assistant stage manager, dramaturge, and actor across 7 mainstage productions; International Thespian Society troupe president with 5 individual Superior Distinctions at state and district. Lead critic for a South Florida high school theater criticism program, 6 pieces published in the Sun-Sentinel, Top Critic nominee. Co-founded the school's first online newspaper and edits two sections. AIPAC high school summit and Capitol Hill lobbying. Congressional Youth Cabinet with her district's congressman. Model UN. Paid summer camp counselor, staff of the year.
interests: Theater in every form: performing, dramaturgy, stage management, and reviewing it professionally. Writing and journalism. Politics and international affairs. Jewish life and youth leadership at international scale. Says she's undecided on a major and half-serious about an eventual PhD.
personality: Full throttle, and organized about it: international board leadership, seven productions, and a newspaper she co-founded, all at once. Quick, articulate, and persuasive like her attorney mother. Happiest with an audience or a byline. Motivation has never been the problem; overcommitment is.
parent1_profession: Physician, medical director of his own practice
siblings: Younger brother, 15 (10th grade), and younger sister, 9. Her brother starts college the year she'll be a senior, so they overlap for one year, which we hear matters for financial aid.
relocation: Anywhere in the country for the right school.
assets: Home owned outright in Boca Raton, roughly $900K. Comfortable retirement savings. No dedicated 529.
college_budget: Honestly unsure. We can pay a meaningful amount, but we'd like merit money to do heavy lifting and we don't know what need-based aid looks like at our income with a paid-off house. Help us figure out what's realistic.
size_preference: Mid-size or larger. She visited a small liberal arts college and it felt claustrophobic within the hour.
geographic_preference: No strong constraint. East Coast is familiar, but anywhere for the right school.
must_haves: A strong Jewish community on campus and real politics and policy opportunities. Great theater and student journalism are nice to have, not requirements.
deal_breakers: Very small schools. Anywhere the culture is wall-to-wall STEM.
schools_on_radar: Yale is the dream and has been for years. Brown and Harvard are also on the list. She ruled out Amherst (too small) and MIT (too STEM-focused). UF isn't off the table, but she'd rather not follow half her high school there; she's ready for somewhere new.
priority_other: The honest question: can she chase real merit money without giving up the Yale shot, or are those two different strategies? Nobody has given us a straight answer.
additional_context: Yale has been the dream for years and she has the profile to try. But we keep hearing that schools like Yale give no merit aid at all, and at $200K with a paid-off house we have no idea what need-based aid we'd actually see, so we are not counting on it. She's a National Merit Semifinalist. Where does real merit money exist for a student like her, and what would it cost her in dream-school terms to chase it? We'd also like to understand whether applying early anywhere helps or hurts the rest of the list. Tell us the truth even if it's uncomfortable.
